questions.question_text: What must all Georgia real estate advertising include regardless of the medium used?
questions.correct_answer: The brokerage firm name as registered with GREC
distractors: The agent's personal license number only
The GREC approval stamp or logo
The listing price and property address
questions.explanation: All Georgia real estate advertising must include the brokerage firm name exactly as registered with GREC. This rule applies to all media, including print, internet, and social media. Omitting the firm name constitutes a blind ad, which is prohibited.

questions.question_text: A Georgia agent posts a property listing on social media using only her personal name and phone number. What violation has occurred?
questions.correct_answer: Running a blind ad without the brokerage firm name
distractors: Failing to include the GREC license number
Advertising without the seller's written consent
Violating the Do Not Call registry rules
questions.explanation: Georgia prohibits blind ads, which are advertisements that do not include the brokerage firm name. Even on social media, every real estate advertisement must identify the firm as registered with GREC.

questions.question_text: A Georgia real estate team creates a website to market listings. Which advertising requirement applies to each page with real estate content?
questions.correct_answer: Each page must identify the brokerage firm
distractors: Only the homepage must identify the firm
Each page must display the team leader's license number
Only pages with active listings need firm identification
questions.explanation: Georgia advertising rules require that every webpage containing real estate advertising identify the brokerage firm. A single disclosure on the homepage is insufficient; each page with real estate content must include the firm name.

questions.question_text: A Georgia team advertises under the team name 'Smith Home Group' but omits the brokerage name. Is this compliant?
questions.correct_answer: No, team ads must always include the firm name
distractors: Yes, registered team names can stand alone
Yes, as long as the team is registered with GREC
Only if the team has its own broker license
questions.explanation: Georgia requires all team advertising to include the brokerage firm name. A team name alone is not sufficient. The firm name must appear as registered with GREC on all advertising materials.

questions.question_text: Georgia's advertising rules for real estate licensees apply to which types of media?
questions.correct_answer: All media including internet and social media
distractors: Print media and television only
Paid advertising only, not organic posts
Only media regulated by the FCC
questions.explanation: Georgia advertising rules apply to all forms of media without exception, including newspapers, websites, social media, yard signs, and business cards. The requirement to display the brokerage firm name is universal.

questions.question_text: How long must a Georgia broker retain transaction records after a closing?
distractors: 5 years from the closing date
7 years from the listing date
1 year from the closing date
questions.explanation: Georgia brokers must retain all transaction records for 3 years from the closing date. This includes listings, contracts, trust account records, correspondence, disclosures, and closing statements.

questions.question_text: A Georgia broker closes a transaction on June 15, 2025. What is the earliest date she may dispose of the transaction file?
distractors: December 31, 2027
questions.explanation: Georgia requires 3 years of record retention from the closing date. A transaction closing June 15, 2025 must be retained through at least June 15, 2028, with the earliest disposal on June 16, 2028.

questions.question_text: Which of the following documents must a Georgia broker retain as part of the 3-year requirement?
questions.correct_answer: Trust account records and closing statements
distractors: Only the signed purchase agreement
Only documents the buyer signed at closing
Only the listing agreement and commission split
questions.explanation: Georgia's record retention requirement covers all transaction documents: listings, contracts, trust account records, correspondence, disclosures, and closing statements. Brokers cannot selectively retain only some documents.

questions.question_text: GREC notifies a Georgia broker that it will audit her transaction files. When can GREC conduct this audit?
questions.correct_answer: At any time without advance scheduling
distractors: Only during the annual license renewal period
Only after a formal consumer complaint is filed
Only with 60 days written notice to the broker
questions.explanation: GREC has the authority to audit broker transaction records at any time. Brokers must maintain organized records and be prepared for unannounced audits. This enforcement power helps ensure compliance with Georgia real estate law.

questions.question_text: A Georgia broker is closing her business and wants to destroy old files. A transaction closed 2 years ago is in the stack. Can she shred this file?
questions.correct_answer: No, it must be retained for at least 3 years
distractors: Yes, she may destroy files when closing the business
Yes, if she transfers the license to inactive status
Only if GREC grants written permission first
questions.explanation: The 3-year retention requirement continues even if the broker closes her business. A file from 2 years ago must be kept for at least one more year. Destroying it prematurely violates Georgia real estate regulations.

questions.question_text: Which level of government has primary zoning authority in Georgia?
distractors: The Georgia state legislature directly
Regional planning commissions appointed by the governor
The Georgia Department of Community Affairs
questions.explanation: Zoning authority in Georgia rests with county and city governments. The Georgia Zoning Procedures Act establishes the framework, but local governments make and enforce specific zoning decisions for their jurisdictions.

questions.question_text: A property owner in Savannah wants to use a residentially-zoned parcel for a small office. What should she apply for?
questions.correct_answer: A variance or special use permit from the local board
distractors: A state zoning override from GREC
A blanket rezoning through the General Assembly
An eminent domain waiver from the county
questions.explanation: To use property in a manner not permitted under current zoning, the owner can apply for a variance (requiring hardship) or a special/conditional use permit from the local zoning board. Public hearings are required before changes are approved.

questions.question_text: Before a Georgia county can change its zoning map, what procedural step is required under the Zoning Procedures Act?
questions.correct_answer: Public hearings must be held before any changes
distractors: A statewide referendum must approve the change
GREC must review and certify the proposed change
Adjacent property owners must give written consent
questions.explanation: The Georgia Zoning Procedures Act requires public hearings before zoning changes can be adopted. This ensures affected property owners and community members have an opportunity to participate in land use decisions.

questions.question_text: A developer wants to build a dock on marshland in coastal Georgia. Which law specifically regulates this activity?
distractors: The Georgia Clean Water Act
The Riparian Rights Preservation Statute
The Federal Wetlands Zoning Ordinance
questions.explanation: The Coastal Marshlands Protection Act protects Georgia's coastal marshlands from unauthorized development. Projects in these areas require permits and are subject to strict environmental review in affected coastal counties.

questions.question_text: A Georgia county takes private property for a new highway. Under the Georgia Constitution, what must the county provide?
questions.correct_answer: Just compensation to the property owner
distractors: A replacement property of equal size
Relocation expenses but no payment for the land
Market value minus outstanding property taxes
questions.explanation: Article I, Section III of the Georgia Constitution requires just compensation when the government takes private property through eminent domain. The owner must receive fair payment for the property's value at the time of the taking.
